content.text: Profiling and performance analysis in agentic AI systems answer one practical question above all others:

In almost every agent deployment the dominant cost is not Python CPU — it is waiting on large language model inference, sequential HTTP tool calls, or repeated embedding lookups. Applying measurement discipline before writing any optimization code prevents teams from spending days tuning code paths that contribute under one percent of real user latency.

Profiling in the context of agentic AI means systematically measuring where an agent spends time and memory across the full execution of a turn — from the moment a user message arrives to the moment the final response is produced. Unlike classical software profiling focused on pure CPU call counts, agent profiling spans distributed system boundaries: the LLM API, external tool HTTP endpoints, vector database queries, and local Python orchestration all contribute to end-to-end latency, and each must be measured independently to isolate the bottleneck.
There are three distinct profiling layers, each answering a different diagnostic question.
(OpenTelemetry spans) answers: what fraction of wall time does each component consume?
(cProfile, py-spy) answers: which Python functions run most often or accumulate the most CPU time?
(memray, tracemalloc) answers: where is heap memory allocated, and are there growth or leak patterns? Together these three layers form a complete observability stack for agent performance.

— exercising the agent under realistic concurrent sessions to determine throughput capacity, worker pool requirements, and queue depth behavior. A single-turn profile under zero load can look very different from the same code serving twenty concurrent sessions that all hit shared rate-limited external APIs simultaneously.

Agent latency is directly user-facing and directly correlated with cost. A turn taking 25 seconds drives abandonment. A turn calling the LLM three times when one call would suffice triples token spend. Without measurement, engineers apply intuition about what to optimize — and intuition reliably targets the wrong component.

A representative breakdown of a 20-second agent turn might look like: LLM inference 14 s (70%), sequential tool HTTP 4 s (20%), retrieval and embedding 1.5 s (7.5%), Python orchestration 0.5 s (2.5%). Optimizing the Python orchestration to zero — a potentially difficult engineering effort — saves 2.5% of latency. Parallelizing the two serial tool calls to overlap saves 2 s (10%). Switching to streaming LLM responses reduces perceived latency by the full 14 s. Profiling makes this arithmetic visible before any optimization work begins.

Cost follows the same logic. If an agent makes three separate LLM calls when one call with a richer prompt would accomplish the same result — a pattern easy to introduce during iterative development — every redundant call burns tokens and API budget. Tracing each LLM call as a named span with token count attributes makes redundancy auditable in the dashboard without any manual inspection of logs.
— identifies the single largest bottleneck before any code is written, preventing wasted effort on low-impact paths.
— capturing latency baselines in CI and alerting on changes catches performance regressions before users encounter them.
— p95 turn latency combined with expected peak concurrent sessions determines worker pool sizes, replica counts, and API rate-limit quota requirements.
— token counts, LLM call frequency, and cache hit rates appear as span attributes, making per-turn cost fully auditable without separate billing API polling.
— profiling before and after a change (adding parallelism, a cache, or context compression) confirms whether the change actually moved the numbers.
— sudden increases in p95 latency or token count per turn in production surface agent regressions (e.g. prompt bloat from a new retrieval path) that have no error signal.

— cProfile adds 10–30% CPU overhead; OpenTelemetry spans add a few hundred microseconds each; memray adds 2–4x memory overhead. Always sample in production rather than collecting everything at full fidelity.

— CPU profilers measure clock time on the thread, not time spent awaiting I/O. An async agent blocked on LLM HTTP shows nearly zero CPU, making CPU profiles uninformative unless run on deliberately CPU-bound paths or with mocked I/O.

— cProfile hooks every function call via sys.setprofile, which changes execution timing enough that results in highly concurrent code may not reflect production. py-spy's sampling approach avoids this but may miss infrequent short functions.

— setting up OpenTelemetry, a collector, and an APM backend adds operational complexity. For small internal tools not yet serving real traffic, the return on investment may not justify the setup cost.
— tracking CPU time while the actual user experience is dominated by time-to-first-token leads to correctly optimizing the wrong dimension. Metric choice must map directly to the user-facing SLO.
— full span tracing at 100% sampling in high-throughput deployments produces gigabytes per hour and non-trivial APM vendor egress costs. Sampling strategies are required at scale.

— a named, timed unit of work with a start timestamp, end timestamp, and a key-value attribute bag. Spans form parent-child trees: the root span represents the full agent turn, and child spans represent individual steps. The wall-clock duration of the root span is what the user experiences. Child spans partition that time across components, making proportional cost visible at a glance.
A span tree for a single agent turn might look like the following. The APM dashboard renders this as a horizontal waterfall chart where each row is a span and width is proportional to duration:

agent.turn            20.0 s
  llm.complete         14.2 s  tokens_in=2400  tokens_out=310
  tool.search           3.1 s  query="risk factors in clause 4"
  tool.fetch            2.4 s  url="https://..."
  retrieve.embed        0.8 s  model="text-embedding-3-small"
  Python overhead       0.5 s

Spans that should overlap (parallel tool calls) appear side by side in the waterfall when truly concurrent and stacked when accidentally serialized. This visual immediately reveals whether an intended
is actually running in parallel or whether a bug has serialized it.
is Python's built-in deterministic profiler. It hooks into every function entry and exit via
, recording a call count and cumulative time for every (caller, callee) pair. The output is a
object sortable by cumulative time, total time, or call count. Tools like
render this as an interactive icicle diagram (inverted flame graph) in the browser.

py-spy is a sampling profiler that attaches to a running Python process via the OS ptrace interface and periodically reads its call stack without modifying the target. Overhead is typically under 1%, and it works in production without code changes. The tradeoff is statistical accuracy: infrequent short functions may be undersampled, but hot paths accumulating many samples are always visible.

memray intercepts Python's memory allocation functions at the C level using LD_PRELOAD and records every allocation and deallocation, producing an exact allocation flame graph indexed by call stack. This pinpoints which function or data structure is responsible for peak memory usage. It is most useful during document ingestion, embedding batch generation, or retrieval index loading — any phase that loads large data structures before iterating.

is a lighter stdlib alternative. It captures allocation tracebacks and supports snapshot comparison — taking a snapshot before and after a suspected leak and diffing them to find surviving allocations. It does not produce flame graphs but adds negligible overhead and works without any installation.
The metrics that matter for agent SLOs are different from those for typical web services because the dominant latency source is external inference, not database queries:
— delay from user request to first streamed token; the primary perceived latency signal for streaming agents, as users see progress immediately once streaming begins.
— total wall time from request to complete response, including all tool calls.
— the 95th percentile of TTFA across all turns; far more meaningful than average because tails determine SLO breaches and user experience for unlucky sessions.
— input plus output tokens, normalized per query to detect unexpected context growth from prompt engineering changes.
— total and per-turn; unusually high counts indicate looping behavior, redundant invocations, or an agent that has become stuck.
— for embedding and retrieval caches; low rates indicate missed batching opportunities or an ineffective cache key strategy.
The relationship between concurrent agent sessions, turn duration, and required worker count is given by Little's Law:
is arrival rate in turns per second, and
is average turn latency in seconds. Profiling provides
workers are sufficient without queue buildup. A 20% headroom over

The diagram below shows how the three profiling layers — distributed tracing, CPU profiling, and memory profiling — relate to the agent turn execution and converge in an APM dashboard for analysis and action.

attrs.alt: Agent turn profiling architecture: span waterfall, CPU profiler, memory profiler feeding APM dashboard with optimization decision tree

content.text: The span waterfall at the top of the diagram represents a representative 20-second agent turn. The LLM completion span (blue) dominates, consuming roughly 60–75% of wall time. Independent parallel tool calls (green) follow. Retrieval and embedding (amber) runs concurrently with tool work when the agent is well-optimized. The thin purple bar represents Python orchestration overhead — usually the smallest component and the last one to optimize. All three profiling tools (OpenTelemetry, cProfile/py-spy, memray) feed into the APM dashboard, which drives the optimization decision tree at the bottom.
Profiling is not a one-time activity. Different techniques apply at different development stages and under different symptoms:
— instrument all agent turns in production from the earliest deployment. The overhead is low and the data is the only reliable basis for performance decisions.
CPU profiling when Python is the suspect
— reach for cProfile or py-spy only after traces confirm that Python orchestration consumes more than 10–15% of turn time, or when mocked-I/O benchmarks are slow.
Memory profiling for ingestion and batch jobs
— apply memray or tracemalloc during document chunking, embedding generation, or any job that loads large files. Rarely needed for online serving unless OOM errors occur.
— simulate expected peak concurrency to validate worker pool size, rate-limit headroom, and queue behavior before real users encounter saturation.
— after adding parallelism, a cache, or context compression. Without re-measurement, 'improvements' may shift the bottleneck without reducing total latency.

Situations where profiling effort should be deferred or minimized: early prototype stage before the tool call graph is stable; when latency is comfortably within SLO with margin; and when volume is too low to produce statistically meaningful percentile data.

# tracer_setup.py
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter


def init_tracing(service: str = "agent", endpoint: str = "http://localhost:4317") -> trace.Tracer:
    provider = TracerProvider()
    exporter = OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint=endpoint)
    provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter))
    trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
    return trace.get_tracer(service)


tracer = init_tracing()

import asyncio, time
from tracer_setup import tracer


async def run_agent_turn(user_message: str, session_id: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent.turn") as turn_span:
        turn_span.set_attribute("session.id", session_id)
        turn_span.set_attribute("input.chars", len(user_message))
        t0 = time.perf_counter()

        # LLM call
        with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm.complete") as llm_span:
            response = await llm_client.complete(
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
                model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
            )
            llm_span.set_attribute("tokens.input", response.usage.input_tokens)
            llm_span.set_attribute("tokens.output", response.usage.output_tokens)

        # Parallel tool calls
        tool_calls = parse_tool_calls(response)
        if tool_calls:
            with tracer.start_as_current_span("tools.parallel_gather"):
                results = await asyncio.gather(
                    *[invoke_tool_with_span(tc) for tc in tool_calls]
                )

        turn_span.set_attribute("turn.latency_ms",
                                (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000)
        return build_response(response, results if tool_calls else [])


async def invoke_tool_with_span(tc) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span(f"tool.{tc.name}") as span:
        span.set_attribute("tool.name", tc.name)
        result = await execute_tool(tc)
        span.set_attribute("result.chars", len(str(result)))
        return result

# Method A: cProfile output to file, inspect with pstats
python -m cProfile -o agent_run.prof scripts/repro_turn.py
python -c "
import pstats
p = pstats.Stats('agent_run.prof')
p.sort_stats('cumulative')
p.print_stats(25)
"

# Method B: snakeviz interactive browser
pip install snakeviz
snakeviz agent_run.prof

# Method C: py-spy — attach to running process, no restarts required
py-spy record -o profile.svg --pid $(pgrep -f "python agent_server")
# opens profile.svg flame graph in browser
Step 4 — memory profile an ingestion job

pip install memray

# Record allocations for a batch job
memray run -o ingestion.bin python ingest_documents.py --corpus ./docs/

# Generate interactive HTML flame graph
memray flamegraph ingestion.bin          # outputs ingestion.html

# Print allocation summary to terminal
memray stats ingestion.bin

pip install locust

# locustfile.py  (see Examples section for full content)
locust -f locustfile.py \
       --headless \
       -u 20 \         # 20 concurrent users
       -r 2  \         # 2 new users per second (ramp-up)
       --run-time 120s \
       --host http://localhost:8080

# Watch p50/p95/p99 latency and failures in the terminal
A minimal decorator records wall time for async functions without the full OpenTelemetry stack, useful during early development and unit benchmarks.

import time, functools, logging
from typing import Callable, Any

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def timed(label: str | None = None):
    """Decorator: logs wall time for any async function."""
    def decorator(fn: Callable) -> Callable:
        name = label or fn.__qualname__

        @functools.wraps(fn)
        async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs) -> Any:
            t0 = time.perf_counter()
            try:
                return await fn(*args, **kwargs)
            finally:
                ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
                log.info("%-40s %7.1f ms", name, ms)

        return wrapper
    return decorator


# Usage
@timed("llm.complete")
async def call_llm(messages: list[dict]) -> str:
    ...  # real LLM call

@timed("tool.web_search")
async def web_search(query: str) -> list[dict]:
    ...  # real search call


# Quick multi-run benchmark utility
async def benchmark(coro_fn, *args, runs: int = 10) -> dict:
    """Run an async function N times and return latency percentiles."""
    times = []
    for _ in range(runs):
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        await coro_fn(*args)
        times.append((time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000)
    times.sort()
    return {
        "p50_ms":  times[runs // 2],
        "p90_ms":  times[int(runs * 0.9)],
        "p99_ms":  times[min(int(runs * 0.99), runs - 1)],
        "min_ms":  times[0],
        "max_ms":  times[-1],
        "runs":    runs,
    }
Example 2 — parallel tool calls with bounded concurrency
Sequential tool calls are the most common and easiest-to-fix performance bug in agent code. This example shows the before and after, with a semaphore to cap downstream API pressure.

import asyncio, time
from opentelemetry import trace

tracer = trace.get_tracer("agent")

# Simulated external search API (1.5 s per call)
async def search_api(query: str) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(1.5)
    return f"results for: {query}"


# ── SLOW: sequential calls  (N × 1.5 s) ─────────────────────────────────────
async def gather_sequential(queries: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    results = []
    for q in queries:
        with tracer.start_as_current_span("tool.search"):
            results.append(await search_api(q))
    return results


# ── FAST: parallel calls with semaphore  (~1.5 s regardless of N) ──────────
_SEARCH_SEM = asyncio.Semaphore(8)   # cap concurrent tool calls

async def _search_bounded(query: str) -> str:
    async with _SEARCH_SEM:
        with tracer.start_as_current_span("tool.search") as span:
            span.set_attribute("query", query[:80])
            return await search_api(query)

async def gather_parallel(queries: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("tools.parallel_gather"):
        return list(await asyncio.gather(
            *[_search_bounded(q) for q in queries]
        ))


# ── Demo ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
async def main():
    queries = [
        "agent memory systems",
        "RAG retrieval patterns",
        "LLM fine-tuning approaches",
        "vector database benchmarks",
    ]

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    await gather_sequential(queries)
    print(f"Sequential: {(time.perf_counter()-t0)*1000:.0f} ms")
    # ≈ 6000 ms  (4 × 1500 ms)

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    await gather_parallel(queries)
    print(f"Parallel:   {(time.perf_counter()-t0)*1000:.0f} ms")
    # ≈ 1500 ms  (all 4 overlap)

asyncio.run(main())

A document question-answering agent deployed for a legal research team was receiving complaints about slow responses on complex queries. The initial measurement with OpenTelemetry showed p95 turn latency at 28 seconds, well above the 15-second SLO. The span waterfall for a representative slow turn revealed:

agent.turn              28.2 s  (p95)
  llm.complete           15.1 s  ← 54 %  (expected)
  retrieve.embed          6.8 s  ← 24 %  (UNEXPECTED — should be < 1 s)
  retrieve.ann_search     2.1 s  ←  7 %
  tool.case_lookup        3.4 s  ← 12 %  (3 databases, sequential)
  Python orchestration    0.8 s  ←  3 %

The embedding step at 24% was the surprise. Investigation revealed that the agent re-embedded the user query on every retrieval call — including follow-up clarification turns in the same session — even when the query had not materially changed. An embedding cache keyed on the SHA-256 of the query text reduced embed time from 6.8 s to 0.1 s on hits. The observed cache hit rate reached 73% after the first turn of each session.
span was running three sequential database queries against separate legal databases. Converting to

cut 3.4 s to 1.2 s. After both changes, p95 dropped to 16.8 s — close but still above SLO. A cProfile run (with real I/O mocked to return instantly) showed 0.7 s in a JSON deserialization loop for case metadata. Replacing

saved 0.4 s, bringing p95 to 16.4 s. The remaining gap was addressed by reducing the system prompt from 3,200 to 1,900 tokens, which reduced LLM TTFT by approximately 1.5 s. Final p95: 14.9 s — within SLO.

# The embedding cache — the highest-ROI single change
import hashlib

_embed_cache: dict[str, list[float]] = {}

async def embed_with_cache(text: str, model: str = "text-embedding-3-small") -> list[float]:
    key = hashlib.sha256(f"{model}:{text}".encode()).hexdigest()
    cached = _embed_cache.get(key)
    if cached is not None:
        with tracer.start_as_current_span("retrieve.embed") as span:
            span.set_attribute("cache.hit", True)
        return cached

    with tracer.start_as_current_span("retrieve.embed") as span:
        span.set_attribute("cache.hit", False)
        vec = await embedding_client.embed(text, model=model)

    _embed_cache[key] = vec
    return vec
Profiling with mocked I/O and reporting wrong conclusions

When LLM calls or tool calls are mocked to return instantly, cProfile shows Python function overhead as large percentages of total time. This causes teams to optimize code that contributes nothing to real latency. Always confirm which component dominates on real network calls before acting on CPU profiles. Use traces as the first diagnostic, CPU profiles only when traces indicate Python CPU as a meaningful fraction.
Using a CPU profiler on async code

# WRONG: cProfile on async code shows event loop internals, not real work
import cProfile, asyncio

async def agent_turn():
    await asyncio.sleep(2.0)   # simulates 2 s LLM call
    return "done"

# cProfile output: asyncio internals dominate, NOT "LLM call"
cProfile.run('asyncio.run(agent_turn())')

# RIGHT: use OpenTelemetry to attribute wall time by operation name
with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm.complete"):
    response = await llm_call()
# The span records the full await duration including network wait

The first request after process startup triggers model weight loading (for local inference), HTTP connection pool initialization, module imports, and JIT compilation. Including cold-start requests in p95 measurements inflates latency figures that do not represent steady-state user experience. Warm up the server with several synthetic requests before recording performance baselines.

# WRONG: spending a day optimizing Python JSON parsing
# while LLM inference dominates at 75% of turn time
def fast_parse(raw: bytes) -> dict:
    return orjson.loads(raw)   # 5 ms → 0.5 ms  (saves 4.5 ms of 20 000 ms turn)

# RIGHT: first confirm which layer is dominant in the span waterfall,
# then pick the optimization that addresses that layer
# LLM latency → context compression / streaming / model routing
# Tool latency → asyncio.gather / batching / caching
# Retrieval    → ANN index / embedding cache / pre-filter
# Python CPU   → cProfile → algorithmic fix (only if > 10% of turn)

A common mistake after fixing one bottleneck is to declare success without re-measuring. Removing a 6 s embedding overhead often reveals that the next component (say, sequential tool calls at 3.4 s) was previously hidden in the shadow of the dominant bottleneck. The optimization loop is always: measure, fix the largest item, re-measure.
. Consistent naming enables dashboard queries, alerts, and cross-agent comparisons.
Attach token counts as span attributes
. These are the primary cost driver and must be visible alongside latency.
Set SLOs on p95, not average
— averages hide tail latency; define and alert on p95 (or p99 for critical flows).
— use 10–20% head-based sampling for cost control plus 100% tail sampling (keep all slow or errored spans) for maximum diagnostic value.
— adding parallelism, caching, or model switching shifts the bottleneck. What was dominant before may no longer dominate after.
— store p50/p95 numbers as artifacts alongside code so regressions are catchable by comparison rather than memory.
Separate single-turn profiling from load testing
— a single-turn profile under zero load gives function-level breakdown; load testing under N concurrent sessions reveals capacity limits and queue behavior.
Never attach raw user text to span attributes
— attach counts, lengths, and hashes only; raw content may contain PII and will be exported to third-party APM backends.

For most agent platforms the throughput ceiling is not Python CPU but external API rate limits and the economics of LLM inference. Scaling strategies focus on concurrency management and reducing per-turn LLM token consumption rather than on traditional compute optimization.
Using Little's Law with profiled p95 latency and expected peak concurrent users gives the minimum worker count:
worker-seconds of capacity. With workers each handling one turn at a time, that means 10 workers. Add 20–30% headroom for burst arrivals.
→ enable streaming to improve perceived TTFT; compress context via summarization or retrieval relevance filtering; route low-complexity turns to a smaller, faster model.
for independent calls; use batch APIs where available (embed 32 chunks in one call instead of 32 individual calls).
→ use ANN indices (HNSW, IVF) instead of exhaustive search; pre-filter by metadata to reduce candidate set; cache embedding vectors for recurring queries.
→ implement token bucket rate limiting per API key; spread load across multiple keys or regions; use exponential backoff with jitter, not fixed retry delays.
→ bound conversation history to a rolling window (last N turns or last K tokens); stream document chunks during ingestion rather than loading whole files into memory.
Token count controls both cost and latency of every LLM call. Transformer self-attention has quadratic complexity in sequence length:

is model dimension. A context of 10,000 tokens may be 5–10× slower to process than one of 3,000 tokens, independent of output length. Retrieval-augmented agents should retrieve the minimum number of chunks necessary and summarize prior context aggressively to keep
— a simple dict or Redis store keyed on SHA-256 of (model, text) eliminates re-embedding repeated queries. Most effective in multi-turn sessions where the first turn's query context recurs.
— cache the top-k chunk list for a query hash. Appropriate only when the underlying corpus is stable; invalidate on any corpus update.
— many providers (including Anthropic) offer prompt caching for long system prompts. Mark stable system-prompt prefixes for caching to reduce both latency and cost on the prompt prefix tokens.
— caching a web search or database write result and replaying it is dangerous. Cache only reads, and always validate TTL against data freshness requirements.
— vendor-neutral instrumentation standard; export to any backend via the OTLP protocol. The standard for new agent code. Python SDK:
— open-source LLM observability platform with first-class LLM span attributes (model, tokens, cost per call). Self-hostable with Docker.
— full observability platform with trace search, dashboards, and anomaly detection. Dedicated LLM Observability product with automatic cost tracking.
— open-source distributed tracing backend; ideal for local development with a single Docker image. No account required.
— open-source OTel auto-instrumentation for LLM SDKs including Anthropic, OpenAI, and LangChain. Reduces boilerplate to one

# OpenLLMetry: one-line auto-instrumentation for Anthropic SDK calls
from traceloop.sdk import Traceloop

Traceloop.init(app_name="legal-agent", api_key="your-langfuse-key")

# All subsequent anthropic.Anthropic().messages.create() calls are
# automatically traced with model, input/output tokens, latency, and cost
— Python stdlib; zero install; use for initial CPU investigation on isolated scripts.
— sampling profiler, attaches to a live process; suitable for production CPU investigation without restarts. Install:
— browser-based interactive icicle/flame viewer for cProfile output. Install:
— Bloomberg's Python memory profiler; produces exact allocation flame graphs. Current standard for Python memory investigation. Install:
— Python stdlib; lightweight; suitable for snapshot-based leak detection. No install required.
— Python-based load testing framework with async support; write agent scenarios as Python coroutines; real-time web dashboard.
— JavaScript-based load testing tool; good for testing REST endpoints at scale; CI integration via GitHub Actions.
— benchmarks individual Python functions in the CI pipeline with statistical stability checking.
Different profiling approaches measure different dimensions of performance. Choosing the right tool avoids the classic mistake of measuring CPU time when the bottleneck is network I/O.
Wall time per operation across all boundaries
Production latency attribution across LLM, tools, retrieval
Misattributes async I/O wait; too heavy for production
Live production CPU investigation without restarts
May miss infrequent short functions; requires ptrace
Ingestion jobs, OOM debugging, allocation flame graphs
Not useful for latency; too heavy for online serving
Less detail than memray; no real-time view
Throughput, queue depth, p99 under load
Capacity planning, SLO validation before launch
Coarse wall time for specific blocks
No hierarchy, no aggregation, no APM integration

Span attributes are exported to third-party APM backends over the network. User query text, retrieved document content, and tool results may contain personally identifiable information or confidential business data. Never attach raw text content to span attributes in production. Attach only counts, lengths, and hashed identifiers:

# WRONG: raw text leaks to APM vendor
span.set_attribute("input.text", user_message)
span.set_attribute("tool.result", str(result))

# RIGHT: attach metadata only
import hashlib
span.set_attribute("input.chars", len(user_message))
span.set_attribute("input.hash", hashlib.sha256(user_message.encode()).hexdigest()[:16])
span.set_attribute("tool.result_chars", len(str(result)))

Linux capability to attach to a running process. In containerized environments this means the profiler container must be launched with elevated privileges. Restrict this capability to designated debug pods and never include it in general production workload definitions. A process running under ptrace has full read access to the target's memory, including in-flight secrets and API keys.

Full tracing at 100% sampling in high-throughput deployments can produce gigabytes of span data per hour and significant APM vendor egress costs. Use 10% head-based sampling for routine operation; apply 100% tail-based sampling (retain all spans from slow or errored traces) for maximum diagnostic value on anomalous requests. A per-route sampling configuration allows critical paths to be sampled at higher rates than routine health checks.

Benchmark profiler overhead explicitly before enabling in production. As a rule of thumb: OpenTelemetry span creation adds under 1 ms per span and is safe at 100% sampling. cProfile adds 10–30% CPU overhead and must not run continuously in production — use it only in targeted debug sessions against a single replica. memray intercepts all allocations and is appropriate only for batch jobs or dedicated debug instances, never for online serving.

Profiling data from a single run is subject to noise from OS scheduling, garbage collection pauses, and JIT warmup. For production baselines, aggregate over hundreds or thousands of turns and use percentiles (p50, p95) rather than single-run measurements. For CPU profiles, run the same workload five to ten times and look for consistent hotspot patterns rather than treating any single profile as authoritative.

Think of an agent turn as an hourglass. The sand is time, and it flows through multiple narrowing points: the LLM API call is the widest and slowest constriction, controlling how fast most sand can fall. Tool HTTP calls are narrower constrictions that may be parallel or serial. Python orchestration is the tiny channel at the very bottom — making it wider has almost no effect on how fast the whole hourglass empties. Profiling maps the width of each constriction, and optimization widens only the ones that actually hold back the flow.
— always measure wall-time span breakdown before writing optimization code; most agent bottlenecks are LLM or network wait, not Python CPU.
OpenTelemetry spans are the primary tool
— they attribute time across LLM, tool, retrieval, and Python boundaries with low overhead, and feed directly into APM dashboards for ongoing monitoring.
Parallel tool execution is the highest-ROI fix
often delivers 2–4× speedup on the tool layer at near-zero engineering cost.
Embedding and retrieval caches eliminate a common hidden cost
— re-embedding the same query across turns is a widespread and invisible waste; a simple dict or Redis cache removes it for all subsequent turns.
— apply cProfile or py-spy only after traces confirm that Python CPU is a material fraction of turn time (more than 10%); do not start there.
Set SLOs on p95 latency, not average
— averages hide tails; design worker pool capacity from p95 turn latency via Little's Law:
Token count drives both cost and latency
